content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 10, 1976

Virginia Mae Days
Director
Department of Veteran's Affairs
Post Office Box 1559
Sacramento CA 95807

Dear Director Days:

This letter is in response to your letter of June 29,
1976, requesting advice with respect to whether certain
members of the California Veterans Board have conflicts of
interest which require disqualification from participation
in certain board decisions. As was indicated to Howell
Jackson, the lack of specific factual information means that
this letter necessarily must be limited to outlining the
standard for determining whether a conflict of interest may
exist, and cannot be used to provide a definitive resolution
of the questions you have presented.

With this limitation in mind, the general facts,
as we understand them, are as follows: Members of the California Veterans Board are required to be veterans but need
not represent any other constituency, interest or back-
ground; the board has the authority to determine all policies
for operation of the Department of Veterans Affairs; in
addition, the board is empowered, in conjunction with the
Veterans Finance Committee, to set a uniform interest rate
for Cal-Vet loans; the board also may determine the amount,
if any, of reserve funds which must be maintained by the
department unavailable for lending; the board also has the
power to set policies with respect to life, disability, fire
and casualty insurance for all Cal-Vet loan holders; one
board member is a director in a savings and loan association
which makes home loans; another board member is an attorney
who represents an insurance company which provides some of
the aforementioned types of insurance.

Government Code Section 87100 provides:

No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 · EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 · ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Virginia Use Days
August 10, 1976
Page Two


Initially, we observe that members of the Veterans Board are
"public officials" within the meaning of Government Code
Section 87100. See Government Code Sections 3004, 3049 2
Cal. Adm. Code Sections 1219 and 18700(a)(1). In addition,
pursuant to the policy making powers of the Board, we think
that board members make and participate in the making of
governmental decisions. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700.
Accordingly, a member of the Veterans Board will be required
to disqualify himself from making, participating in the
making, or using his official position to influence any
board decision in which he has a financial interest.

Government Code Section 87103 states that:

    An official has a financial interest in a decision
    within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is rea-
    sonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
    material effect, distinguishable from its effect
    on the public generally, on:

    (a) Any business entity in which the public
    official has a direct or indirect investment worth
    more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

    (b) Any real property in which the public
    official has a direct or indirect interest worth
    more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

    (c) Any source of income, other than loans
    by a commercial lending institution in the regular
    course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
    dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
    promised to the public official within twelve
    months prior to the time when the decision is
    made; or

    (d) Any business entity in which the public
    official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
    employee, or holds any position of management.
    For purposes of this section, indirect investment
    or interest means any investment or interest owned
    by the spouse or dependent child of a public offi-
    cial, by an agent on behalf of a public official,
    by any business entity controlled by the public
    official or by a trust in which he has a substan-
    tial interest. A business entity is controlled by
    a public official if the public official, his
    agents, spouse and dependent children hold more
    than fifty percent of the ownership interest in
    the entity. A public official has a substantial
    interest in a trust when the official, his spouse
    and dependent children have a present or future
    interest worth more than one thousand dollars
    ($1,000).

Virginia Mae Davis
August 10, 1970
Page Three


2 Cal. Adm. Code section 19702 sets forth guidelines for
determining when a decision has a material financial effect.
It would our conclusion, however, that the statutes set
forth in 2 Cal. Adm. Code section 19702 are only guidelines
and that, as the regulation provides, the ultimate question
is whether the existence of the official's financial inter-
est might interfere with the performance of his duties "in
an impartial manner free from bias."

With the foregoing as a framework, we have concluded that it is possible that the two board members de-
scribed in your letter will be required to disqualify them-
selves from participating in the making of certain board
decisions. However, as we indicated before, due to the lack
of specific factual information, we are unable to determine
which decisions of the board will, in fact, foreseeably have
a material financial effect on a board member's financial
interests.

With respect to the board member who is a director
with a savings and loan association, we think that in particu-
lar, disqualification should be carefully considered before
he participates in decisions which affect (1) the interest
rate of Cal-Vet loans and (2) the amount of funds held in
reserve. It is our understanding that the interest rate on
Cal-Vet loans is lower than the interest rate on similar
loans from private lending institutions. However, in return
for this lower interest rate, the Cal-Vet program imposes
restrictions on borrowers which generally are not present in
loans from private lending institutions. Under these circum-
stances, the closer the interest rate of the Cal-Vet program
is to that imposed by private lending institutions, the more
likely it is that the borrower will attempt to obtain his
loan from a private institution. Accordingly, if it is
reasonably foreseeable that a decision on the Cal-Vet interest
rate will produce enough new borrowing from private lending
institutions to materially affect the savings and loan associa-
tion of which the board member is a director, he should
disqualify himself from all participation in that decision.
Moreover, he should refrain from using his official position
to influence any other board member relative to such a decision.

We also understand that the board determines the
amount of funds, if any, which must be held in reserve un-
available for loans. This reserve fund un-
doubtedly affects the amount of money available for Cal-Vet
loans. Moreover, if there are insufficient funds available
to meet the demand for Cal-Vet loans from eligible borrowers,
needy borrowers are given priority over other applicants.

Virginia Mae Days
August 10, 1975
Page Four

Pursuant to this procedure, it is possible that a savings
and loan association will acquire new borrowers by virtue of
the Western Savings Association on the sites of the Cal-Vet
reserve fund. If it is foreseeable that a decision on the
reserve fund will create enough new borrowers to have a
material financial effect on the savings and loan association in question, the board member who is a director of that
association must disqualify himself from participation in
any such decision.

The attorney who represents an insurance company
which provides insurance through the Cal-Vet program also
may be required to disqualify himself from participation in
certain board decisions. If the attorney holds a 10% or
more interest in his law firm, the clients of the firm (including the insurance company) which provide at least $250 a
year of income to the firm are sources of income to the
attorney within the meaning of Government Code section 87103(c).
See Government Code section 82030. If, on the other hand,
the attorney does not have at least a 10% interest in his
law firm, then the law firm, not the insurance company, will
be his source of income under Government Code section 87103(c).
In this latter situation, it is unlikely that the attorney
will have to disqualify himself from board decisions since
it is doubtful that decisions affecting the insurance company
will in turn have a material financial effect on the law
firm.

In the former situation, however, where the attorney
does hold a 10% interest or greater in the law firm disqualification is a distinct possibility. With respect to many
decisions (for example, decisions relative to premium amounts,
interest rates on reserves maintained by the insurance company
which eventually may be returned to the Department of Veterans
Affairs, the scope of insurance coverage and escape clauses),
it might be reasonably foreseeable that there will be a
material financial effect on the insurance company and, if
so, disqualification will be required. We want to reiterate,
however, that in the absence of specific facts it is impossible to provide advice as to which decisions will fall into
this category.

Finally, we want to emphasize that in each instance
it is not sufficient to show a material financial effect on
the insurance or savings and loan industry in general rather, the test is whether a material financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable with respect to the particular financial
interest of the board member in question. However, if such
a material financial effect is foreseeable, disqualification

Virginia Bar Lays
August 10, 1979
Page Five

may not be avoided by suggesting that such an effect is
approximately the same for all savings and loan or insurance
companies, and thus, not distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally. An industry, trade, or profession
represents the public generally only in narrowly prescribed
circumstances, and these circumstances are not present in
the situations to which you have alluded. See 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 13703.

I hope the foregoing is helpful. If you have any
further questions, please contact Ted Prim, an attorney with
the Commission's Legal Division.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Michael Bennett
Executive Director

WBPLh
cc: Attorney General

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

DEPARTMENT OF VETERANS AFFAIRS
POST OFFICE BOX 1559, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95807

Telephone:  (916) 445-6437

June 29, 1976


Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
Chief, Conflicts of Interests Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814


     Re:  Request for Informal Written Opinion.


Dear Mr. Spurlock:

     I would like an informal written opinion on the following
questions:

     1.  Would a member of the California Veterans
Board, who is also a member of the board of directors of
a savings and loan association, violate Section 87100 of
the Government Code by participating in decisions
establishing the interest rates to be paid on Cal-Vet
loans?

     2.  Would a member of the California Veterans
Board, who is also a member of a law firm which represents
certain insurance companies doing business with the
Department, violate Section 87100 of the Government Code
by participating in policy decisions affecting the
Department's relationship with such companies?

     The California Veterans Board consists of seven members
appointed by the Governor subject to confirmation of the
Senate. Mil. & Vet. C. §65.  Each member is required to
be a "veteran," Mil. & Vet. C. §66, and is appointed for a
four-year term.  Mil. & Vet. C. §67.  Each member receives
a per diem of $20.00 for each day's attendance at a board
meeting, and is reimbursed for his necessary traveling and
other expenses.  Mil. & Vet. C. §68.


                    HIRE A VETERAN - HIRE EXPERIENCE

Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
June 29, 1976
Page Two                        Request for Informal Written Opinion


The Board is charged with determining policies for all
operations of the Department. Mil. & Vet. C. §72. In
addition, the Board is empowered, by a two-thirds vote
of its members and with the approval of the Veterans
Finance Committee of 1943, to establish a uniform rate
of interest to be paid by veterans on their Cal-Vet
loans, and to raise or lower the rate of interest as
many times and as frequently as it deems to be in the
best interests of the Department and the Cal-Vet
loanholders. Mil. & Vet. C. §§987.17a and 987.87.

One of the members of the Board is also a director of a
large federal savings and loan association, and another
is a member of a law firm representing insurance
companies with which we do business. Two large insurance
companies underwrite life and disability insurance for
all Cal-Vet loanholders, and one large company presently
underwrites the fire and casualty insurance on Cal-Vet
homes.

I suppose that the answer to these questions depend upon
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that an interest
rate decision or a policy decision on insurance would
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on the member's
business entity or source of income, respectively.

It seems at least conceivable that a higher interest rate
on Cal-Vet loans could result in more veterans borrowing
from savings and loan associations, or that a policy
decision increasing insurance benefits could result in
premium increases with corresponding increases in the
insurance companies incomes.

If you need any additional information, please let me know.

Very truly yours,

VIRGINIA MAE DAYS
Director

VMD/bjk

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

DEPARTMENT OF VETERANS AFFAIRS
POST OFFICE BOX 1559, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95807

Telephone:  (916) 445-3111


July 2, 1976


Mr. Iver Skjeie
Office of the Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 550
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re:  Request for Opinion on Possible Incompatibility

Dear Mr. Skjeie:

A question has been raised for a possible conflict under Government Code
Section 87100 of two of our Veterans Board members participation in the
Cal-Vet Farm and Home Purchase interest rate recommendation and insurance
aspects of the Board's duties.  A copy of our letter to the FPPC regarding
these questions is enclosed.

If such a conflict exists, is there an incompatibility as it relates to
service on the Board, particularly with regard to the interest rate
recommendation which is an important and regular duty of the Board?  Also,
regardless of the conflict statutes generally and the provisions of Government
Code Section 87100 specifically, is there an incompatibility?

We discussed this briefly on June 30, 1976 with Mr. John Gordnier and he
suggested we address the question to you.  He suggested that we include a
brief outline of the method by which interest rates are set for Cal-Vet
Farm and Home Purchases and who has purchased the Cal-Vet bonds together
with a description of our insurance programs and how insurance companies
were selected.  We have enclosed this information.

Since we do not yet have disclosure statements we can tell you no more than
is included in the June 29, 1976 letter to FPPC other than that the Federal
Savings and Loan of which our Board member is a director has not purchased
the Cal-Vet Bonds and the insurance company represented by a Board member's
law firm is unknown.

A prompt reply, even in the form of an informal oral opinion followed up by
one in writing would be appreciated.  I understand the Legislative Counsel
may have given an opinion regarding the Savings and Loan directorship but
I am not sure when that was done.

Very truly yours,


VIRGINIA MAE DAYS
Director

cc:  Agriculture and Service Agency
     John Gordnier        HIRE A VETERAN - HIRE EXPERIENCE
     Delbert L. Spurlock Jr.

OUTLINE OF METHOD FOR INTEREST RATES FOR CAL-VET AND DESCRIPTION OF INSURANCE
PROGRAM AND SELECTION OF COMPANIES.

The Cal-Vet loan interest rate is established by a 2/3 vote of the California
Veterans Board with the approval of the Veterans Finance Committee.(Military
and Veterans Code Section 987.17 and 987.17 a). Annually the sufficiency of
the Cal-Vet loan rates are reviewed by staff of the Division of Farm and Home
Purchases based upon the actual cost of general obligation bond sales and an
increment for administstcy costs, taking into consideration the current value of
money and the solvency of the Veterans Farm and Home Building Fund. The
Director then makes a recommendation to the Board which they may or may not
follow.

Sales of Veterans Bonds are held after a request for the sale from the Director
of Veterans Affairs is approved by the Governor and the Veterans Finance
Committee has met and has authorized the State Treasurer toschedule the sale
at a time considered most advantageous. Sales are by sealed bid. (Military
and Veterans Code Section 998.008, 998.010).

No bond sales were held in 1969. The following are dates of sales from 1970
to the present time showing the manager of the syndicate that was the successful
bidder for the bonds. The interest rate is bid on each Veterans Bond according
to its year of maturity.

     August 1970                    Bank of America
     December 1970                  1st National City Bank
     June 1971                      1st National City Bank
     January 1972                   Bank of America
     October 1972                   1st National City Bank
     October 1973                   Bankers Trust
     March 1974                     Bank of America
     August 1974                    United California Bank
     November 1974                  Bank of America
     April 1975                     United California Bank
     March 1976                     Chase Manhattan Bank

The Home Protection Plan of Life Insurance has been in existence since 1938
and has been jointly underwritten by the California Western States Life
Insurance Company and Occidental Life Insurance Company since that date.
Disability insurance was added in 1955. At least once each year an actuarial
forecast is made by the underwriting insurance companies as to the sufficiency
of the premium rates to support the insurance plans and a report is made to
the Director of Veterans Affairs.

Since July 1, 1975, fire and hazard insurance has been provided under a single
policy insuring all Cal-Vet properties except condominium units by St. Paul
Fire and Marine Insurance Company. Between December 1, 1970 and July 1, 1975,
the carrier was Hartford Fire Insurance Company. For ten years prior to that
coverage was provided by the National American Insurance Company. The contract
in 1970 with Hartford Fire Insurance Company and the 1975 contract with St. Paul
Fire and Marine Insurance Company were awarded on sealed bids arranged by the
Insurance Office of the Department of General Services.

Catastrophe insurance, which is a supplement to the Disaster Indemnity Fund
was negotiated through five brokerage firms. These are Emett & Chandler,
Fred S. James & Company, Dinner Levison Company, Marsh & McLennan, Inc., and
Pickett-Rothholz Company. The brokers canvassed the market to obtain this
policy as this kind of coverage had very limited availability. The State
Insurance Officer handled this also.

-2-

Even though insurance companies have changed, there are some claims still in process
with the other insurance companies. Disputes do arise as to which company
is responsible for recovery of the damages involved.

The Board member that may have a problem connected with the Savings and Loan
directorship was appointed to the Board in 1969. The Board member with the
law firm handling insurance was appointed in 1971.

Would a member of the California Veterans 
Board, who is also a member of the board of directors of 
a savings and loan association, violate Section 87100 of 
the Government Code by participating in decisions 
establishing the interest rates to be paid on Cal-Vet

appointed by the Governor subject to confirmation of the 
Senate. 
Mil. & Vet. C. §65. 
Each member is required to 
be a "veteran," Mil. & Vet. C. §66, and is appointed for a 
four-year term. 
Mil. & Vet. C. §67.

a board 
meet 
,and is reimbursed for his necessary traveling and 
other expenses.

Two large insurance 
companies underwrite life and disability insurance for

Very tr~ly yours,

sections.conclusion: We have concluded that it is likely that the two board members described in your letter will be required to disqualify themselves from participating in the making of certain board decisions. However, as we indicated before, due to the lack of specific factual information, we are unable to determine which decisions of the board will, in fact, foreseeably have a material financial effect on a board member's financial interests.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether California Veterans Board members who have financial interests (as savings and loan directors or insurance company attorneys) violate Government Code Section 87100 by participating in board decisions affecting Cal-Vet loans, interest rates, reserves, and insurance policies.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that board members with financial interests in savings and loan associations or insurance companies will likely need to disqualify themselves from certain decisions, particularly those affecting Cal-Vet loan interest rates, reserve funds, and insurance policies, based on whether a material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (June 29, 1976 letter from Department of Veterans Affairs) and FPPC response (August 10, 1976 advice letter). OCR quality degraded in places but key sections extracted. Response addresses three main scenarios: savings and loan director, law firm partner representing insurance company, and general standards under Gov. Code §87100 and §67103.

Board, who is also a member of the board of directors of 
a savings and loan association, violate Section 87100 of 
the Government Code by participating in decisions 
establishing the interest rates to be paid on Cal-Vet 
loans?

certain insurance companies doing business with the

Department's relationship with such companies? 
The California Veterans Board consists of seven members 
appointed by the Governor subject to confirmation of the 
Senate. 
Mil. & Vet. C. §65. 
Each member is required to 
be a "veteran," Mil. & Vet. C. §66, and is appointed for a 
four-year term. 
Mil. & Vet. C. §67.

a board 
meet 
,and is reimbursed for his necessary traveling and 
other expenses. 
Mil. & Vet. C. 
~68.

June 29, 1976

of interest to be paid by veterans on their Cal-Vet

Mil. & Vet. C. §§987.17a and 987.87.

July 2, 1976 
Mr. Iver Skjeie 
Office of the Attorney General 
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 550 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Re: 
Request for Opinion on possible Incompatibility 
Dear Mr. Skjeie: 
A question has been raised for a possible conflict under Government Code 
Section 87100 of two of our veterans Board members participation in the 
Cal-Vet Farm and Home Purchase interest rate recommendation and insurance 
aspects of the Board's duties. 
A copy of our letter to the FPPC regarding 
these questions is enclosed. 
If such a conflict exists, is there an incompatibility as it relates to 
service on the Board, particularly with regard to the interest rate 
recommendation which is an important and regular duty of the Board? 
Also, 
regardless of the conflict statutes generally and the provisions of Government 
Code Section 87100 specifically, is there an incompatibility? 
We discussed this briefly on June 30, 1976 with Mr. John Goranier and he 
suggested we address the question to you. 
He suggested that we include a 
brief outline of the method by which interest rates are set for Cal-Vet 
Farm and Home Purchases and who has purchased the Cal-Vet Bonds together 
with a description of our insurance programs and how insurance companies 
were selected. 
We have enclosed this information. 
Since we do not yet have disclosure statements we can tell you no more than

the Cal-Vet Bonds and the insurance company represented by a Board member's 
law firm is unknown. 
A prompt reply, even in the form of an informal oral opinion followed up by 
one in writing would be appreciated. 
I understand the Legislative Counsel 
may have given an opinion regarding the Savings and Loan directorship but 
I am not sure when that was done.

CONCLUSION: We have concluded that it is likely that the two board members described in your letter will be required to disqualify themselves from participating in the making of certain board decisions. However, as we indicated before, due to the lack of specific factual information, we are unable to determine which decisions of the board will, in fact, foreseeably have a material financial effect on a board member's financial interests.

BOX 607 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 August 10, 1976 Virginia Mae Days Director Department of Veteran's Affairs Post Office Box 1559 Sacramento CA 95807 Dear Director Days: This letter is in response to your letter of June 29, 1976, requesting advice with respect to whether certain members of the California Veterans Board have conflicts of interest which require disqualification from participation in certain board decisions. As was indicated to Howell Jackson, the lack of specific factual information means that this letter necessarily must be limited to outlining the standard for determining whether a conflict of interest may exist, and cannot be used to provide a definitive resolution of the questions you have presented. With this limitation in mind, the general facts, as we understand them, are as follows: Members of the California Veterans Board are required to be veterans but need not represent any other constituency, interest or back- ground; the board has the authority to determine all policies for operation of the Department of Veterans Affairs; in addition, the board is empowered, in conjunction with the Veterans Finance Committee, to set a uniform interest rate for Cal-Vet loans; the board also may determine the amount, if any, of reserve funds which must be maintained by the department unavailable for lending; the board also has the power to set policies with respect to life, disability, fire and casualty insurance for all Cal-Vet loan holders; one board member is a director in a savings and loan association which makes home loans; another board member is an attorney who represents an insurance company which provides some of the aforementioned types of insurance. Government Code Section 87100 provides: No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 · EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 · ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441 Virginia Use Days August 10, 1976 Page Two Initially, we observe that members of the Veterans Board are "public officials" within the meaning of Government Code Section 87100. See Government Code Sections 3004, 3049 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 1219 and 18700(a)(1). In addition, pursuant to the policy making powers of the Board, we think that board members make and participate in the making of governmental decisions. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700. Accordingly, a member of the Veterans Board will be required to disqualify himself from making, participating in the making, or using his official position to influence any board decision in which he has a financial interest. Government Code Section 87103 states that: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is rea- sonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
embedding.summary: The FPPC provides guidance on conflict of interest standards under Government Code Section 87100 for California Veterans Board members with financial interests in savings and loan associations and insurance companies, concluding that disqualification will likely be required for certain decisions but declining to specify which ones without more factual detail.
